title: Apigee X for Spring Boot Developers
subtitle: Learn Apigee X by bridging every concept to Spring — through UK Open Banking & FAPI
author: Apolaki edition

parts.title: Part 1 — Foundations & the Apigee mental model
parts.theme: What an API-management product is, and why you configure it instead of coding it.

parts.title: Part 2 — Flow & mediation engine
parts.theme: The request/response flow model and the policy toolkit that does the real work.

parts.title: Part 3 — Security, identity & API products
parts.theme: Threats, the entitlement chain, OAuth, JWT, mTLS, and shared flows.

parts.title: Part 4 — FAPI 1.0 Advanced & the OB trust framework
parts.theme: The Open Banking ecosystem and the FAPI hardening profile, requirement by requirement.

parts.title: Part 5 — Building the UK Open Banking APIs
parts.theme: DCR, consent, AISP reads, PISP payments, and confirmation of funds — end to end.

parts.title: Part 6 — Operations, delivery & observability
parts.theme: Environments, CI/CD config-as-code, analytics, and productisation.

parts.title: Part 7 — Capstone
parts.theme: Assemble a production-grade Open Banking platform and run a go-live review.

1.title: What an API-gateway product actually is
1.objective: Explain what an API-management product is and why Apigee X is configured, not coded — contrasting it with a Spring Cloud Gateway you would hand-write and operate yourself.
1.bridge: Spring Cloud Gateway is a gateway you code and deploy; Apigee X is a gateway product you configure and operate.
1.stretch: List three cross-cutting concerns each of your current Spring services re-implements that a gateway product would centralise.

2.title: Architecture & your free evaluation org
2.objective: Map Apigee's control-plane / runtime split and the org → environment → environment group → instance hierarchy, then provision a free evaluation org.
2.bridge: Think Spring profiles and deployment targets — but as first-class platform objects you manage, not application config.
2.stretch: Diagram how your app's dev/staging/prod profiles map onto Apigee environments and environment groups.

3.title: Your first passthrough reverse proxy
3.objective: Create, deploy, and call a passthrough reverse proxy that fronts a backend target, and read the proxy bundle that defines it.
3.bridge: Like a @RestController that just forwards to a downstream service — except there is no code, only a proxy bundle.
3.stretch: Point the proxy at one of your own Spring Boot services via a tunnel and call it through Apigee.

4.title: Revisions, deploy & rollback
4.objective: Use proxy revisions to deploy, promote, and roll back changes the way you move immutable container image tags.
4.bridge: A proxy revision is an immutable artifact like a tagged container image; deploying is choosing which tag runs.
4.stretch: Script a one-command rollback to the previous revision and prove it with a curl before and after.

5.title: The Trace tool is your gateway debugger
5.objective: Use the Apigee Debug/Trace tool to inspect a live request flow the way you step through a Spring request with a debugger.
5.bridge: Trace is your breakpoint-and-watch view for the gateway: every policy, variable, and branch, captured per request.
5.stretch: Capture a Trace session, export it, and annotate where each policy mutated the message.

6.title: The flow model & request/response symmetry
6.objective: Master Apigee's flow model — ProxyEndpoint and TargetEndpoint, PreFlow / conditional Flows / PostFlow — and the four points where logic attaches.
6.bridge: It looks like a Spring filter chain, but it is symmetric: request and response each have their own pipeline on both the proxy and target sides.
6.stretch: Map a HandlerInterceptor's preHandle/postHandle/afterCompletion onto Apigee's four attach points and note what does not line up.

7.title: The message & flow-variable model
7.objective: Understand the message object and flow variables — the typed, request-scoped context every policy reads and writes.
7.bridge: Flow variables are like a request-scoped Map<String,Object> (think ServletRequest attributes), but with a fixed schema and dotted names.
7.stretch: Enumerate the built-in variables available after VerifyAPIKey and compare them to what your Spring SecurityContext exposes.

8.title: Conditions, RouteRules & conditional flows
8.objective: Route and branch with conditions, RouteRules, and conditional flows instead of if/switch logic in controller code.
8.bridge: RouteRules are declarative routing predicates — like Spring Cloud Gateway predicates or @RequestMapping conditions, but evaluated as flow conditions.
8.stretch: Express one of your RequestMappingHandlerMapping rules as an Apigee conditional flow.

9.title: AssignMessage & ExtractVariables
9.objective: Read and reshape requests and responses with ExtractVariables and AssignMessage — the core mediation policies.
9.bridge: This is your gateway's equivalent of a request/response @ControllerAdvice body rewriter — but declarative.
9.stretch: Reproduce a Spring ResponseBodyAdvice transformation entirely in AssignMessage.

10.title: JavaScript vs Java callouts: the trade-off
10.objective: Choose between JavaScript policies and Java callouts for custom logic, understanding the performance and packaging trade-offs.
10.bridge: Like choosing between a lightweight script and a packaged @Component bean — except one runs in a sandboxed JS engine and the other as a JAR.
10.stretch: Port a small piece of Spring service logic into a JavaScript policy and measure the Trace timing.

11.title: Quotas & SpikeArrest: distributed and product-scoped
11.objective: Protect backends with distributed Quota and SpikeArrest, scoped to API products rather than per-instance counters.
11.bridge: Unlike Bucket4j or Resilience4j running per-pod, Apigee quotas are distributed and counted across the whole runtime, per product.
11.stretch: Compare a Resilience4j RateLimiter config to an equivalent SpikeArrest + Quota and note the semantic differences.

12.title: Caching vs KVM vs PropertySets: the three stores
12.objective: Pick the right store — ResponseCache, Key-Value Maps, or PropertySets — for cached data, mutable config, secrets, and read-only properties.
12.bridge: Resist the 'just add a database' reflex: these map to caching, externalised config (@ConfigurationProperties), and read-only property files.
12.stretch: Move a hard-coded application.yml value into a PropertySet and read it from a policy.

13.title: Threat protection, CORS & data masking
13.objective: Block malformed and malicious payloads, enable CORS safely, and mask sensitive data in traces and logs.
13.bridge: Centralises what you would otherwise scatter across Spring Security filters, @CrossOrigin, and log-scrubbing config.
13.stretch: Replace a Spring CorsConfigurationSource with an Apigee CORS policy and verify preflight behaviour.

14.title: API Products, Apps, Developers & keys
14.objective: Model who may call your APIs with the Developer → App → API Product → key entitlement chain, and enforce it with VerifyAPIKey.
14.bridge: There is no clean Spring equivalent — it is closer to an OAuth client registry plus an entitlements/plans table you would otherwise build yourself.
14.stretch: Map your current API-client onboarding process onto the developer/app/product chain and find what you would delete.

15.title: OAuthV2 as a token server (client credentials)
15.objective: Turn Apigee into an OAuth 2.0 token server issuing and verifying client-credentials tokens with the OAuthV2 policy.
15.bridge: Apigee becomes your Authorization Server — the role you would otherwise stand up with Spring Authorization Server.
15.stretch: Issue a token from Apigee and validate it in a downstream Spring resource server with a JWT decoder.

16.title: Auth-code + PKCE & JWT verification
16.objective: Implement the authorization-code + PKCE flow and verify JWTs for stateless, signed identity at the edge.
16.bridge: The flow your Spring oauth2Login() client consumes — now you are building the server side of it.
16.stretch: Wire a Spring oauth2Login client against your Apigee auth-code endpoint end to end.

17.title: TLS & mTLS: keystores, north- & south-bound
17.objective: Secure both edges with one-way and mutual TLS using keystores and truststores, north-bound and south-bound.
17.bridge: Like configuring a server.ssl keystore plus a client RestTemplate truststore — but for both edges of the gateway at once.
17.stretch: Configure mTLS between Apigee and a backend Spring service using a self-signed client certificate.

18.title: TargetServers, load balancing & health
18.objective: Decouple backend hostnames with TargetServers, and add load balancing and health monitoring.
18.bridge: TargetServers are externalised, environment-scoped backend definitions — like Spring Cloud LoadBalancer instances, but as platform config.
18.stretch: Define two TargetServers with health checks and watch failover happen in a Trace.

19.title: Shared flows, FlowHooks & the fault taxonomy
19.objective: Extract cross-cutting logic into shared flows attached via FlowHooks, and design a predictable fault and error taxonomy.
19.bridge: Shared flows + FlowHooks are your gateway's OncePerRequestFilter applied platform-wide; FaultRules are your @ExceptionHandler.
19.stretch: Refactor three duplicated policies into one shared flow attached by a FlowHook.

20.title: The OB trust framework & OBIE roles
20.objective: Map the UK Open Banking trust framework — ASPSP / AISP / PISP / CBPII roles, the Directory, and eIDAS / OB certificates.
20.bridge: This is the regulated domain model around your APIs, not a Spring concept — the ecosystem your gateway must enforce.
20.stretch: Place your organisation in the OBIE role map and list which certificates you would need.

21.title: The FAPI Advanced profile: what it requires
21.objective: Read the FAPI 1.0 Advanced profile and map each requirement — PAR, signed request objects, mTLS-bound tokens, JARM — to an Apigee mechanism.
21.bridge: FAPI is a hardening spec layered on the OAuth you already know; each clause becomes a concrete policy, not framework magic.
21.stretch: Produce a requirement-to-policy traceability table for the FAPI Advanced clauses.

22.title: Implementing FAPI I: PAR, request objects & client auth
22.objective: Implement Pushed Authorization Requests, signed request objects, and private_key_jwt / mTLS client authentication.
22.bridge: You are building the strict server-side validations a Spring FAPI client relies on — JWS verification, not just @Valid.
22.stretch: Reject a tampered request object in a Trace and capture the exact fault that fires.

23.title: Implementing FAPI II: mTLS-bound tokens & introspection
23.objective: Issue and enforce certificate-bound access tokens and implement token introspection per FAPI.
23.bridge: Token binding ties the bearer token to the client certificate — closing the stolen-token gap a plain Spring resource server leaves open.
23.stretch: Prove a cert-bound token fails when replayed from a different client certificate.

24.title: Dynamic Client Registration (DCR)
24.objective: Onboard TPPs automatically by validating Software Statements and registering clients via Dynamic Client Registration.
24.bridge: Self-service client registration as an API — the automated version of the manual app onboarding from session 3.2.
24.stretch: Register a client from a sample SSA and read back the created developer app.

25.title: The account-access consent lifecycle
25.objective: Build the account-access consent lifecycle — create, authorise, enforce, and revoke a consent resource.
25.bridge: A consent is a stateful resource with its own lifecycle — model it like a JPA entity state machine, enforced at the gateway.
25.stretch: Diagram the consent state machine and enforce rejection of one illegal transition.

26.title: AISP reads: accounts, balances & transactions
26.objective: Expose Accounts, Balances, and Transactions endpoints that enforce consent and FAPI headers.
26.bridge: The read APIs map to your @GetMapping resources — but authorization is consent-scoped, not role-scoped.
26.stretch: Enforce that a token may only read the accounts named in its consent.

27.title: PISP payments & idempotency
27.objective: Implement domestic payment consent and submission with idempotency keys and risk data.
27.bridge: Idempotency keys are the gateway-level version of the dedupe logic you would hand-roll in a Spring payment service.
27.stretch: Prove that replaying a payment with the same x-idempotency-key returns the original result.

28.title: Confirmation of funds & the end-to-end journey
28.objective: Add Confirmation of Funds (CBPII) and assemble the full consent → authorise → pay → confirm journey.
28.bridge: The capstone of the domain APIs — composing the resources you built into one regulated journey.
28.stretch: Run the full AIS + PIS + CoF journey as a single scripted end-to-end test.

29.title: Environments, env groups & hostnames
29.objective: Promote proxies across environments and route traffic with environment groups and hostnames.
29.bridge: Externalised, platform-managed environments and routing — the grown-up version of Spring profiles plus an ingress.
29.stretch: Promote a proxy from dev to test with no bundle change, only environment configuration.

30.title: CI/CD & config-as-code
30.objective: Automate build, test, and deploy of proxies as config-as-code with apigeecli and the Maven plugin.
30.bridge: Your proxy bundle becomes a versioned artifact in a pipeline — exactly like your Spring app's CI/CD, applied to gateway config.
30.stretch: Add a GitHub Actions job that deploys a proxy revision on merge to main.

31.title: Observability, analytics & Advanced API Security
31.objective: Use Analytics, custom reports, and Cloud Logging for observability, and survey Advanced API Security.
31.bridge: Apigee Analytics is the API-traffic counterpart to your Micrometer / Actuator metrics, but built in at the edge.
31.stretch: Build a custom report for FAPI error rates broken down by API product.

32.title: Productisation, monetization & API hub
32.objective: Package and publish APIs, survey monetization, and catalogue APIs in API hub.
32.bridge: The productisation layer with no Spring analogue — turning your endpoints into consumable, governed products.
32.stretch: Draft a product bundle and rate plan for your AISP API.

33.title: Capstone: a production-grade OB platform & go-live review
33.objective: Assemble consent + AISP + PISP + CoF into one FAPI-secured Open Banking platform and run a go-live readiness review.
33.bridge: The full system — every Spring-bridge concept composed into one operated, regulated platform.
33.stretch: Run a subset of the Open Banking conformance suite against your build.
